meta.title: chron

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF. Parts, glosses, context sentences and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: chron is the Bible's own Journal example (§11: 'chron, Greek time: chronic, anachronism, synchronise, chronology'). asynchronous reuses the a- and syn- prefixes so the same pieces resurface across families.

root.label: chron
root.mentor_note: chron hides inside more ordinary words than it first seems to.

attempt.direction_prompt: A brief episode, or a lasting condition?

context_sentences: The disease had become chronic, resisting every treatment the doctors attempted that year.
Chronic underfunding, not any single bad decision, was what had hollowed out the library system.
The museum arranged the artefacts in a strict chronology, from the earliest tools to the latest coins.
Historians still disagree about the exact chronology of events in the weeks before the treaty.
The ship's captain trusted the chronometer more than any other instrument on board.
A single faulty chronometer was enough to send the entire survey team off course.
The two departments worked in an asynchronous rhythm, one always finishing hours after the other had begun.
Because the meeting was asynchronous, colleagues on three continents could each reply in their own evening.

construct_options.text: Happening in exactly the wrong order

context_sentences: The old chronograph on his wrist could time a race down to a fifth of a second.
Collectors still pay a premium for a chronograph built before the war.

construct_options.text: A device that records time with great precision
A drawing that shows how something changes over a year
A calendar used to plan future events
